coredata.dc:title: Old silver mirror in qualitative analysis with new shoots in quantification: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) as fluorescent probes for “off-on” sensing of formalin in food samples
coredata.dc:creator: Naksen, Puttaraksa
Jarujamrus, Purim
Anutrasakda, Wipark
Promarak, Vinich
Zhang, Liyuan
Shen, Wei
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorometric assay for selective and sensitive determination of formalin (FA) was developed based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) coupled with silver mirror reaction. N-CDs was synthesized using the hydrothermal method with the ethylene glycol and ammonia solution as carbon and nitrogen precursors, respectively. The detection principle was based on “off-on” fluorescence switching. Specifically, the fluorescence signal of N-CDs was first turned off after incorporating the Ag+ and Tollens’ reagents. Then, in the presence of FA, the Ag+ species on the N-CDs surface were reduced to Ag0 species and the fluorescence signal of N-CDs was switched back on. The fluorescence intensity due to the N-CDs signal linearly increased with the increasing FA concentrations in the range of 5–100 mg L−1, with the detection limit of 1.5 mg L−1. The proposed approach provides rapid, simple, sensitive, and selective detection of FA in various food samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Formalin (FA)
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs)
Fluorometric assay
Silver mirror reaction
Tollens' reagent

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Parameter optimization for using N-CDs for the formalin determination

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is also supported by the National Research Council of Thailand (IRN/502/2563) chaired by Assoc. Prof. Dr. Duangjai Nacapricha. The research scholarships of the Ubon Ratchathani University.

This work is also supported by the National Research Council of Thailand ( IRN/502/2563 ) chaired by Assoc. Prof. Dr. Duangjai Nacapricha. The research scholarships of the Ubon Ratchathani University.

The research scholarships and instrumental facility of the Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Ubon Ratchathani University, are gratefully acknowledged. Science Achievement Scholarship of Thailand (SAST) given to Puttaraksa Naksen is also gratefully acknowledged. The Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC), Ministry of Higher Education, Science, Research and Innovation is also gratefully acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (insert: size distribution) and (b) FTIR spectra of the N-CDs. Fluorescence spectra of the N-CDs at (c) different excitation wavelengths (310–390 nm) and (d) different pH values (n = 3).

The absorption and fluorescence spectra (excitation wavelength of 330 nm) of N-CDs. (Please refer to the Web version of this article for the interpretation of references to color in this figure legend.). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a), (b) Fluorescence spectra (the excitation wavelength of 330 nm) of (A) DI water, (B) N-CDs, (C) N-CDs-Ag+, (D), (E), and (F) NCDs-Ag+-Tollens’ reagent without FA with 50 and 100 mg L−1 FA, respectively.

TEM images of samples containing N-CDs: (a) N-CDs, (b) N-CDs-Ag+, (c) N-CDs- Ag+- Tollens' reagent and (d)* N-CDs-Ag+-Tollens’ reagent-FA. Absorption spectra of N-CDs, N-CDs-Ag+ and NCDs-Ag+-Tollens’ reagent with and without FA (e). *It is to be noted that the mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min to remove Ag0 precipitate and keep the supernatant solution. Only the supernatant was dropped on 200-mesh Cu grids coated with carbon film.
Linear relationship between the fluorescence enhancement factor and the formalin concentrations (n = 3).
Schematic diagram for the preparation of fluorescent N-CDs and the on-off-on fluorescent switching of N-CDs in the presence and absence of formalin based on the mirror reaction.
The comparison of formalin determination in real samples using the proposed method and the acetylacetone method. (n = 3).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The concentration of FA in real samples were calculated from the linearity graph (Fig. 5).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The concentration of FA in real samples were calculated from the acetylacetone linearity graph (Fig. S9).

head.ce:title: Old silver mirror in qualitative analysis with new shoots in quantification: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) as fluorescent probes for “off-on” sensing of formalin in food samples

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorometric assay for selective and sensitive determination of formalin (FA) was developed based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) coupled with silver mirror reaction. N-CDs was synthesized using the hydrothermal method with the ethylene glycol and ammonia solution as carbon and nitrogen precursors, respectively. The detection principle was based on “off-on” fluorescence switching. Specifically, the fluorescence signal of N-CDs was first turned off after incorporating the Ag+ and Tollens’ reagents. Then, in the presence of FA, the Ag+ species on the N-CDs surface were reduced to Ag0 species and the fluorescence signal of N-CDs was switched back on. The fluorescence intensity due to the N-CDs signal linearly increased with the increasing FA concentrations in the range of 5–100 mg L−1, with the detection limit of 1.5 mg L−1. The proposed approach provides rapid, simple, sensitive, and selective detection of FA in various food samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Formalin (FA) has been classified as human carcinogens and mutagens in laboratory animal testing by the World Health Organization (WHO) [1]. It is the most common aldehydes found in the environment and is present naturally in some foods. In addition, FA is also used for many purposes such as animal preservation [2], textiles [3], furniture [4], cosmetic manufacture [5] and agriculture [6]. Recently, FA has been illegally used to maintain food freshness and storage time [7]. FA intake causes chronic and acute effects on neurological, respiratory and digestive systems [8,9]. In some countries, the FA is permitted as a preservative in some food samples. The presence of FA as a breakdown product of hexamethylenetetramine is permitted in cheeses in Europe to a maximum residue limit (MRL) of 25 mg kg−1 [10]. Given the great variability of FA in foods, a more general MRL has not been set. The use of FA as a preservative for feed is still under discussion at the European level although a concentration of 2500 mg kg−1 is already permitted in the USA [11]. According to the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) [12], daily exposure to FA from food of animal and plant origin should not exceed 100 mg kg−1 food per day. Therefore, FA has been banned from use as a food additive. Nonetheless, the application of FA both as preservatives and odor masking agents in foods has not been uncommon. The development of an accurate, low-cost, and easy-to-use sensing system for the determination of FA levels in food is hence of particular importance.

Various techniques have been used for qualitative and quantitative determination of FA in food including electrochemistry [13], gas chromatography (GC) [14,15] and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [16]. These methods provide effective analytical performance for low concentrations and high selectivity of FA; but require expensive instruments and complicated sample preparation. To overcome these drawbacks, optical sensing has been developed as an alternative method for FA detection in food. This alternative method, widely accepted as a standard method, was proposed for quantitative analysis using acetylacetone [17] and for qualitative analysis via silver mirror reaction [18]. With optical sensors, the sensitivity of the sensors based on the changes in color intensity has been improved and therefore these sensors have been widely used for monitoring formalin. For the semi-quantitative detection, the formalin sensitive dyes or nanomaterials were also applied in colorimetric assays as indicators for an easy detection by the naked eye [19–22]. Various types of semi-quantitative detection kits have been available commercially. Nonetheless, these kits offered relatively low quantitative accuracy for FA determination in food, due to the reliance on the judgement of color intensity by human eye and the potential background interference from colors of foods [23].

To eliminate food color interferences, sample preparation must be employed using various extraction methods that are time-consuming, relatively costly, and involve multi-step processing. These drawbacks have prevented the sensors from being suitable for rapid and cost-effective on-site analysis. In this regard, fluorometry is a promising technique to address this shortcoming. Essentially, fluorometry has recently emerged as a highly sensitive method for formalin determination [24]. As excellent fluorescent materials, carbon-based dots (CDs) which include graphene quantum dot (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and carbon nanodots or carbon dots (CNDs or CDs) are low-cost and environmentally friendly materials for this purpose. In particular, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) has exhibited great potential for the designing stimuli-responsive materials [25,26]. Nitrogen doping not only retains primary properties of CDs such as large specific surface area, quantum size effect, and good biocompatibility, but also brings distinctive optical and electronic characteristics. The electron delocalization and charge-carrier density of N-CDs can be drastically altered by tuning N doping degree, C–N configurations, or surface functional groups. These properties are likely to further endow N-CDs with a greater potential for use as an effective dual/sensor or probe [25,26]. Nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) usually inherit carboxyl, carbonyl, amino, amide, and hydroxyl groups from their precursors.

Recently, a considerable interest has been shown in applying CDs as fluorometric sensing for FA determination. For instance, in 2017, Y. Zhang et al. [27] fabricated a fluorescent nanosensor for formaldehyde detection with N-GQDs and melamine. Under optimal conditions, the photoluminescence (PL) intensity of the N-GQDs/melamine decreased linearly with increasing formaldehyde concentration. The gas sensor showed a linearity range of 0.009–0.9 mg L−1 and the detection limit (LOD) of 0.003 mg L−1. Likewise, H.J. Li et al. [28] developed formaldehyde gas responsive NGQDs encapsulated by N-rich organic species. The sensor provided reversibly “off-on” fluorescence via redox reactions. A wide formaldehyde concentration range of 0.03–3 mg L−1 was detected from this system.

Thereafter, the first lysosome-targeted CDs for ratiometric fluorescence detection of FA were developed from dexamethasone and 1,2,4,5-tetraaminobenzene through a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method by H. Liu et al. [29]. In addition, W. Yang et al. [30] fabricated a new metal-enhanced fluorometric system developed by a combination of silica encapsulated CDs and the silver mirror reaction. In this system, silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) grew in-situ on the surface of fluorescent silica spheres, resulting in a remarkable fluorescence enhancement. The linearity range of 0.01 mg L−1 to 1 mg L−1 was obtained. Nevertheless, the many steps required for sensor preparation, a long reaction time up of to 30 min, a complication of sample preparation for being gas generation before analysis and not validation in the real sample are among the drawbacks of this system.

In this work, the N-CDs were fully synthesized [31] and characterized which were used as highly stable fluorescent probes. A simple and rapid FA quantification based on silver mirror reaction using non-modified N-CDs for “off-on” sensing has been firstly developed in order to further improve the detection efficiency of the qualitative silver mirror method [18]. The novel approach serves as promising a means to quantitatively detect FA in food samples with satisfactory results. The mechanism of the use of N-CDs as fluorescent probe in this work was also investigated. Ultimately, the proposed approach serves as a promising technique for further development of an efficient fluorometric approach for the detection of FA.

All of the solutions used in this work were prepared using deionized (DI) water with 18.2 MΩ resistance from a Millipore Milli-Q purification system (Bedford, USA). All chemicals were of analytical grade and were used as received without further purification. Silver nitrate (AgNO3) was obtained from Fisher Scientific (Hampton, USA). Ethanol (C2H5OH; 99.9%), ethylene glycol (EG; C2H6O2), and hexane (C6H14) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Missouri, USA). Sodium acetate (CH3COONa) and ammonium solution (NH4OH; 25%) were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, US). Real samples (fish, apple, octopus, fingerroot, shrimp, crisp squid, cabbage, bean sprouts and broccoli) were purchased from a local market in Ubon Ratchathani province, Thailand.

The morphology of N-CDs was investigated by transmission electron microscope (TEM; TECNAI G2 20 S-TWIN) with 200-mesh Cu grids coated with carbon film and scanning electron microscope coupled with energy dispersive spectrometer (SEM-EDS, JSM-IT300, JEOL) using a platinum film coated on the specimen. Zeta potential analyzer (Nano ZS, Malvern) and dynamic light scattering (DLS, Nano ZS, Malvern) were used to determine the zeta potential and hydrodynamic size of the N-CDs, respectively. Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Nicolet 6700, Thermo Scientific) was recorded with attenuated total reflectance (ATR) mode in the range of 4000–500 cm−1 for functional group identification on the surface of N-CDs. The photoluminescent emission of the samples were measured by fluorescence spectrometer (model LS55; PerkinElmer, Massachusetts, USA). The absorbance spectra of N-CDs were collected using a double beam UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu, Japan) within a spectral range of 200–800 nm to study the molecular electronic transition. The time-resolved fluorescence lifetime was measured with a time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) device. A picoseconds diode laser (IBH, UK, Nanoled) was used as the light source at the excitation wavelength of 310 nm. The signal was detected at the magic angle (54.7°) polarization using a Hamamastsu MCP PMT (3809U) and the decays were analyzed using IBH DAS-6 decay analysis software. Absolute quantum yield (QY) was measured using Integrating Sphere with FLS980 Edinburgh Instruments. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (AXIS Ultra DLD, Kratos Analytical, Manchester, U.K.) was used for the investigation of elemental composition.

The N-CDs was simply prepared by a hydrothermal method [31]. Briefly, 25 mL of ethylene glycol as a carbon precursor was added into a Teflon-lined autoclave and then heated at 200 °C for 5 h in an oven. After cooling the autoclave to room temperature, a homogeneous solution, designated as carbon dot (O-CDs), was obtained. Then, 25 mL of O-CDs was mixed with 1340 μL of ammonia solution in a beaker (as a nitrogen precursor). After stirring for 30 min, the mixture was transferred to the Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling the autoclave to room temperature, the excess ammonia was eliminated by heating the resultant product at 100 °C for 1 h in air. The yellow solution with N-containing groups was then obtained and designated as N-CDs without the step of dialysis. The concentration of N-CDs was determined to be 2500 mg L−1.

The determination of FA using N-CDs based on the mirror reaction was carried out by the following procedure: first, 30 μL of 2500 mg L−1 N-CDs was mixed with 150 μL of 0.5 M AgNO3 in a 1.5 mL microcentrifuge tube. Next, 6 μL of 0.024 M Tollens’ reagent (NH3 dissolved in NaOH) was added into the mixed solution. Then, each standard solution containing FA or sample solution was added into the mixed solution and incubated for 3 min at room temperature. Finally, the mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min to remove Ag0 precipitate and keep the supernatant solution. The fluorescence spectrum of the supernatant solution was then recorded at the excitation wavelength of 330 nm, and the fluorescence intensity at the emission wavelength of 410 nm was used for the quantitative analysis (Schematic diagram ).

The selectivity of the developed approach for FA over other aldehyde compounds as well as compounds containing other functional groups including benzaldehyde, acetaldehyde, acetone, acetylacetone, n-butanol, glucose and formic acid were evaluated by the following procedure. First, 30 μL of 2500 mg L−1 N-CDs was mixed with 150 μL of 0.5 M AgNO3 in a 1.5 mL microcentrifuge tube. Next, 6 μL of 0.024 M Tollens’ reagent was added into the mixed solution. Then, 30 μL of 50 mg L−1 standard FA solution and 20 μL of 100, 200, 300, 400, 500 and 600 mg L−1 were added into the mixture and it was subsequently incubated for 3 min at room temperature. Finally, the mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min to remove Ag0 precipitate and the supernatant solution was kept for fluorescence analysis.

Food samples including fish, apple, octopus, fingerroot, shrimp, crisp squid, cabbage, bean sprouts and broccoli, purchased from a local market in Ubon Ratchathani province, Thailand, were used for real sample analysis. The preparation of the real samples was performed as follows. First, each sample was cut into small pieces. Then, 5.0 g of each sample was mixed with 50 mL of deionized water, followed by the incubation for 30 min. The mixture was then filtrated through a Whatman No. 1 filter paper twice according to a procedure previously reported [19]. Then, 250 μL of the obtained liquid sample was analyzed using the same procedure reported in Section 2.4.

The FA detection in this proposed method was validated by acetylacetone method [18]. Nash's reagent was prepared by dissolving 15.0 g of ammonium acetate, 0.3 mL of acetylacetone, and 0.2 mL of acetic acid in 100 mL of DI water. Ammonium hydroxide was also used to adjust the pH of the system for being 6.0 to 6.5 before further use. As-prepared Nash's reagent was kept in a dark glass bottle because it's light-sensitive. For the detection, 3.0 mL of as-prepared Nash's reagent was added to 5.0 mL of the FA standards, or the clarified sample contained FA (as mentioned in the section of 2.6) in 25 mL of DI water, followed by heating in a water bath for 15 min at 60 °C. After cooling, the mixture was immediately measured the absorbance at 412 nm, using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer.

The TEM image of N-CDs (Fig. 1 a) shows that N-CDs particles were spherical in shape and exhibited a homogeneous distribution. The size distribution of the particles was estimated using ImageJ software and graphically represented in the inset of Fig. 1a. The mean particle diameter of N-CDs was found to be 2.8 ± 0.3 nm (n = 50). The average hydrodynamic particles size measured by DLS technique was found to be 259.9 ± 37.6 nm. The discrepancy in the average size measured by the DLS and TEM techniques might have resulted from the fact that the surface of N-CDs in the solution was surrounded by other hydrophilic compounds. This may be attributed to the complex surface structure of the N-CDs. Apart from functional groups, the surface of the N-CDs may consist of hydrophilic functional groups that extend when N-CDs are dispersed in DI water. This surface characteristic is usually caused by incomplete carbonization of polymer chains (after dehydration of dissolved ethylene glycol as a precursor) during bottom-up thermal synthesis of N-CDs without the step of dialysis [32], leading to the large hydrodynamic diameter [33,34]. The zeta potential of N-CDs measured by zeta-sizer was found to be −15.8 ± 0.2 mV in deionized water medium, indicating the presence of negatively charged hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of the N-CDs. Essentially, these groups were responsible for enhancing the hydrophilicity and solubility of N-CDs in aqueous solution [23]. Moreover, the presence of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen elements in N-CDs were confirmed by SEM-EDS (Table S1), indicating the successful introduction of nitrogen atom on the surface of N-CDs.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was employed to identify the functional groups of N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1b, N-CDs exhibited a broad OH and NH2 stretching peak at ca. 3300 cm−1. Additional peaks were also found at ca. 1538 cm−1, 1420 cm−1 and 2071 cm−1, attributable to C–N, CO–NH2 and CNH− (or−CN) stretching frequencies, respectively. According to the FTIR analysis, the synthesized N-CDs contained the following functional groups: hydroxyl (-OH), carboxyl (-COOH) and amine (-NH2) groups. In addition, XPS results were used to further investigate the type of element and N type of N-CDs. Particularly, the XPS spectrum of N -CDs can reveal information about O, N and C atoms (Fig. S1). Additionally, the graphitic-N, pyrrolic-N, pyridinic-N and oxidized N were also found in a high resolution XPS spectrum of N 1s where pyrrolic-N was most abundant (Fig. S2(a)).

The fluorescence spectra of 50 mg L−1 of N-CDs using different excitation wavelengths (λ ex) from 310 nm to 390 nm were shown in Fig. 1c. The results show that when λ ex was increased from 310 to 330 nm, the fluorescence intensity also increased and the maximum emission wavelength (λ em) around 410 nm was observed with bright blue photoluminescence for the λ ex of 330 nm. However, when λ ex was further increased to 390 nm, the fluorescence intensity dramatically decreased and λ em was blue-shifted up to 450 nm. It should be noted that, based on the FTIR results, the increase of sp2 carbon domains was likely due to the amination reaction and could enhance the luminescence of N-CDs. Furthermore, the electron-donating amine groups have a better ability for electron donation than hydroxyl groups and can further enhance the luminescence of N-CDs.

The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 2 ) shows a strong absorption peak at around 230 nm and broad shoulders centered at around 280 nm and 330 nm. The peak at 230 nm is due to the transition from π to π* of CC and CN bonds while those at 280 and 330 nm are due to the transition from n to π * of CO bonds [23]. The presence of carboxyl/carbonyl functional groups may be originated from the surface of N-CDs while that of the CC groups was from the sp2 hybridized polymeric domains in the core.

The absolute quantum yield (QY) of N-CDs was found to be 20.7% (Table S2). The absorption edge peak form Tauc Plot was used for determination of energy band gab (Eg) of N-CDs. As demonstrated in Fig. S3, Eg of N-CDs was showed 3.7058 eV correspond to delta energy (ΔE) 3.7449 eV was calculated form the optimum excitation wavelength (330 nm) of N-CDs. That confirm the excitation wavelength at 330 nm was great suitable excitation of N-CDs. Moreover, the energy band gab was corresponding with previous work (Fig. S3) [35].

The synthesis of N-CDs as proposed, which uses hydrothermal method, does not involve complex procedures and can promote high stability and long shelf-life of N-CDs. On the other hand, the microwave method would lead to the presence of residues and therefore require several purification steps (e.g. filtration and centrifuge). Essentially, with the proposed sources of carbon and nitrogen in this work, the solvent in the step of synthesis and further purification steps were not required.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Parameter optimization for using N-CDs for the formalin determination
ce:section.ce:para: The reaction parameters including concentration of silver ions (Ag+), concentration of tollens’ reagent and incubation time were optimized based on the mirror reaction using the synthesized N-CDs for the determination of formalin. First, the Ag+ concentration in the range of 0–0.2 M was studied and the results are shown in Fig. S5. It was found that the fluorescence intensity decreased upon increasing Ag+ concentration and the intensity did not significantly change for the Ag+ concentration of 0.05 M or higher. Due to electron of N-CDs transfer to Ag+ based on PET process [36]. Therefore, 0.05 M was selected as the optimum concentration of Ag+.

In case of the concentration of Tollens' reagent, 0.05, 0.10 and 0.15 mM concentrations were investigated using 50 mg L−1 of N-CDs and 0.05 M of Ag+ (Fig. S6). The results show using 0.10 M Tollens' reagent gave the highest linearity with r2 = 0.9975 in the plot of formalin concentration (0–100 mg L−1) against fluorescence enhancement (I/I0). Therefore, 0.10 mM was selected as the optimal concentration of Tollens’ reagent.

The effect of incubation time from 1 to 30 min was also investigated for 5 mg L−1 and 50 mg L−1 formalin (Fig. S7). The results show that the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was quite constant over the incubation time studied for both concentrations of formalin. Therefore, 3 min was selected as the optimal incubation time.

To better understand how the developed approach works, the mechanism based on the mirror reaction using the synthesized N-CDs for the formalin determination was investigated. The fluorescence spectra of N-CDs based solutions were shown Fig. 3 a and b. The high fluorescence intensity of pure N-CDs (Fig. 3a–A) decreased significantly after the addition of Ag+ (N-CDs-Ag+, Fig. 3a and B). The dramatic quenching (switch-off) after the addition of Ag+ was likely due to the binding of Ag+ ions on the surface of N-CDs. This finding is consistent with the fact that the synthesized carbon dots are highly nitrogen-doped as confirmed by FTIR technique, which result from the use of ammonium solution during the synthesis. The selectivity of carbon dots towards Ag+, as compared with other metal ions, is driven by the flexible coordination sphere of Ag+ [37]. Additionally, the presence of carboxyl (-COOH), hydroxyl (-OH), and amine (-NH2) groups on the surface further enhanced the interaction between the carbon dots and Ag+ [38].

As for the particle sizes of N-CDs-Ag+, TEM analysis indicated that they were not significantly different from those of pure N-CDs (Fig. 4 a and b). In addition, the average hydrodynamic particles size and the zeta potential of pure N-CDs and N-CDs-Ag+ were found to be 259.9 ± 37.55 nm, −15.8 ± 0.153 mV and 242.3 ± 72.84 nm, 11.9 ± 1.68 mV, respectively (Table S3). The change in zeta potential from negative to positive supports the idea of the binding of Ag+ with –COOH, –NH2 and -OH groups on the surface of N-CDs as mentioned above.

Following the addition of Ag+ (N-CDs-Ag+), the fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs decreased from 8.5 to 3.4 ns (Table S4). This finding indicates the transfer of electrons from N-CDs to Ag+ species (Table S4) and suggests that the fluorescence quenching process in N-CDs-Ag+ was likely resulted from the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process [36]. The driving force for the PET process was the energy gap between the LUMO of the N-CDs (anion radical formed) and the LUMO of Ag+ (cation radical formed) as shown in Fig. S3, resulting in the ionic interaction between Ag+ with the hydroxy, carboxylic and amine groups on the surface of the N-CDs (Schematic diagram). Specifically, the intensity of graphitic-N, pyrrolic-N, pyridinic-N and the oxidized N dramatically decreased (Fig. S2 (b)), suggesting that Ag+ was favorably bound to these of N. After Tollens' reagent was added to system, resulting in the N-CDs-Ag+-Tollens’ reagent, the fluorescence intensity remained unchanged as compared to that of N-CDs-Ag+ (Fig. 3b–D, 4c). This result agreed with the zeta potential (Table S3). This phenomenon was likely caused by complexation of Ag+ and NH3 in Tollens’ reagent on N-CDs surface [39].

When 50 mg L−1 and 100 mg L−1 FA were added to the developed approach (N-CDs-Ag+-Tollens’ reagent-FA), the fluorescence intensity was re-enhanced (switch-on) (Fig. 3b–E and 3b-F, Fig. S2(c)). There findings might be due to the reduction of some Ag + species to Ag0 species after the addition of FA while some remained unreacted and still deposited on the surface of N-CDs (Fig. S2(d)). This phenomenon was supported by the decrease of Ag+ observed in UV–Vis spectrum where FA concentration was included (Fig. 4e). The maximum absorbance peak of all step was observed at 300 nm (Fig. 4d) that confirmed Ag+ contain in the solution [40]. Moreover, the absorbance of N-CDs-Ag+, N-CDs-Ag+-Tollens’ reagent in both absence and presence of FA at 300 nm were decreased due to the reduction of Ag + ion to Ag0 precipitate (only a precipitate of Ag0 was formed without the formation of AgNPs (Fig. 4e) [19].

Furthermore, the TEM result of N-CDs-Ag+-Tollens’ reagent-FA showed the large particle of Ag0 with the small particle of N-CDs (Fig. 4d) which close to that of pure N-CDs (Fig. 4a, Table S3). However, the Ag0 precipitates were neglected.

Overall, the results suggest that the N-CDs could ultimately enhance the fluorescence emission based on the mirror reaction. Specifically, the fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs-Ag+-Tollens’ reagent-FA was found to be 3.4 ns, which were very close to that of N-CDs-Ag+-Tollens’ reagent (3.3 ns) (Table S4).

Inspired by the optical properties, the fluorescence of N-CDs-Ag+-Tollens’ reagent and N-CDs-Ag+-Tollens’ reagent-FA (the FA concentration from 5 mg L−1 to 1000 mg L−1) with the excitation wavelength of 330 nm was investigated. The plot of FA concentration against the fluorescence enhancement (I/I0; I and I0 are the fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of formalin, respectively) is shown in Fig. 5 a. A linear relationship was obtained in the FA concentration range of 5–100 mg L−1 with r2 = 0.9977. The limit of detection (LOD) was found to be 1.5 mg L−1 at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. These results indicate that the developed method is sensitive for formalin at low concentration levels which can meet legal limits of various food safety.

Selectivity is a key factor for the efficiency of formalin approach. Under the optimal conditions, the selectivity of the developed approach towards formalin over other aldehyde compounds and compounds containing other functional groups including benzaldehyde, acetaldehyde, acetone, acetylacetone, n-butanol, glucose and formic acid was investigated in terms of tolerance concentration (Table S5). The tolerance concentration is defined as the highest concentration of each interference that could be added to 50 mg L−1 FA solution to maintain the fluorescence intensity within one standard deviation of the experiment without any interference. The results show that the approach had high tolerance towards n-butanol, acetaldehyde, glucose, and acetone with tolerance concentrations of 500 mg L−1, 400 mg L−1, 300 mg L−1 and 300 mg L−1, respectively. A lower tolerance (100 mg L−1) was observed towards benzaldehyde, acetylacetone and formic acid, although still within the acceptable tolerance concentration level. It should be noted that, as opposed to the decent tolerance results in this work, the presence of other aldehydes in FA-containing samples has been identified as a limitation for FA determination in some other methods [41]. In this work, the adequate tolerance for other aldehydes can be attributed to the selectivity of the silver mirror reaction towards FA. Specifically, the selectivity towards FA over other compounds depends on the reducing ability of the interference compounds. FA, according to Adkins' studies [41], possesses the highest oxidation potential among all aldehyde compounds and therefore is the best reducing agent among the studied compounds that can react with Tollens’ reagent as the oxidizing agent, resulting in the aforementioned high selectivity.

The developed approach was further used to investigate the contamination of FA in real samples including fish, apple, octopus, fingerroot, shrimp, crisp squid, cabbage, bean sprouts and broccoli. To evaluate the accuracy of the developed method, each sample was also spiked with certain amounts of standard FA solution and FA concentration was determined by the proposed method in comparison with the unspiked samples. In addition, the results obtained from the developed method were also compared to those measured by the classical acetylacetone method [18].

The results, as shown in Table 1 , indicate that FA was not detectable in all unspiked samples measured by the developed method. In the spiked samples, however, the recovery was found to be in the range of 93.6–107.5%. These findings suggest the high accuracy of the developed method. In case of precision, the percentage of relative standard deviation (%relative error) was less than 10% (n = 3) in all samples.

To validate the results, the FA concentrations in unspiked and spiked samples measured by the developed method were compared to those obtained from the classical acetylacetone method (Table 1). It was found that the results were not significantly different. Additionally, the overall characteristics of the developed method used in this work is also compared to other works using carbon nanodots as a fluorescent probe for formalin determination (Table S7). The details highlight several advantages of the method developed in this study, including selectivity, sensitivity, rapid detection capabilities, low cost-effectiveness, and the ease of operation.

The stability of N-CDs was also evaluated. As shown in Fig. S8, the N-CDs remained stable in the aqua medium for at least 62 days. The high stability is likely caused by the un-reacted ethylene glycol stabilizing and preventing N-CDs from aggregation. Finally, the precision for the determination of formalin concentration using the developed method were also investigated by performing intra-day and inter-day experiments (Table S6). The results show that %RSD of intra-day and inter-day were less than 4.81 and 8.44%, respectively.

A simple, sensitive, selective and rapid fluorescent method for formalin (FA) determination was developed. The method was based on the silver mirror reaction using N-CDs as the fluorescent probe, which exhibited switchable “off-on” fluorescence in the presence and absence of formalin, respectively. The reduction of Ag+ species on the N-CDs surface by FA (fluorescence quenching) is a key step for FA determination. The fluorescence intensity due to the N-CDs signal was found to linearly increased with increasing the FA concentrations in the range of 5–100 mg L−1, enabling the quantitative analysis of FA in real samples. This method showed good selectivity towards FA over other aldehyde compounds and compounds with other functional groups. The synthesized N-CDs also exhibited long-term stability. Moreover, this method was applicable for the determination of FA in food samples with high precision for both inter-day and intra-day experiments. Notably, this developed method also exhibited high accuracy compared to the conventional acetylacetone method. Therefore, the use of N-CDs as fluorescent probe in this work serves as a highly promising technique for the detection of formalin in food samples.
This work is also supported by the National Research Council of Thailand (IRN/502/2563) chaired by Assoc. Prof. Dr. Duangjai Nacapricha. The research scholarships of the Ubon Ratchathani University.

Science Achievement Scholarship of Thailand (SAST) given to Puttaraksa Naksen is also gratefully acknowledged. The Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC), Ministry of Higher Education, Science, Research and Innovation is also gratefully acknowledged.
